summary:
The Battle of Vienna (September 12, 1683 AD) was the decisive turning point of the [[event:2324|Ottoman-Habsburg Wars]], at which a relief army under Jan Sobieski of Poland routed the Ottoman forces besieging Vienna, saving the Habsburg capital and beginning the long Ottoman retreat from central Europe. The Grand Vizier Kara Mustafa had assembled the largest Ottoman army ever sent to Europe — estimated at 140,000–300,000 men — besieging Vienna with 86,000 inside the perimeter. After two months of siege, with the city's walls nearly breached and starvation setting in, the relief army arrived: Sobieski commanded a Holy League coalition of Polish, Habsburg, Bavarian, and Saxonian forces totaling approximately 65,000 men.

The battle's decisive moment came when Sobieski launched the largest cavalry charge in history — approximately 18,000 heavy cavalry (including his famous Polish winged hussars) — down the slopes of the Vienna Woods into the Ottoman camp. The sheer shock of the charge broke Ottoman morale; the Ottoman army, already under pressure from coordinated infantry and cavalry attacks across the entire front, collapsed and fled. Kara Mustafa barely escaped; he was later strangled on the Sultan's orders for the failure. The victory was immediately celebrated across Christian Europe as a providential deliverance. Its long-term consequences were equally dramatic: the Holy League's subsequent campaigns (the Great Turkish War) progressively expelled Ottoman forces from Hungary, Transylvania, and the northern Balkans — territories the Ottomans had held for 150 years — culminating in the [[event:2468|Treaty of Karlowitz]] (1699) that permanently shifted the balance of power between the empires.
